quiz.title: High-School Geometry: Unit 2 Quiz 8
quiz.subtitle: Using Angle Relationships to Establish Parallelism
quiz.subject: Geometry

questions.question: Two lines are cut by a transversal. Which pair of congruent angles is sufficient to prove the lines are parallel?
options: Vertical angles
Adjacent angles
Alternate interior angles
Angles that form a linear pair
questions.explanation: If alternate interior angles are congruent, then the lines are parallel by the converse of the Alternate Interior Angles Theorem. Vertical angles are always congruent regardless of parallelism, so they cannot be used to prove lines parallel.

questions.question: Two lines are cut by a transversal. Which condition is sufficient to prove the lines are parallel?
options: Corresponding angles are supplementary
Consecutive interior angles are supplementary
Vertical angles are congruent
Adjacent angles are congruent
questions.explanation: If consecutive interior angles are supplementary, then the lines are parallel by the converse of the Consecutive Interior Angles Theorem. Vertical angles are always congruent regardless of parallelism.

questions.question: Two lines are cut by a transversal. $m\angle 1 = 65^\circ$ and $m\angle 2 = 65^\circ$. $\angle 1$ and $\angle 2$ are corresponding angles. What conclusion follows?
options: The lines are perpendicular
The lines are parallel
The transversal bisects both angles
No conclusion about the lines can be drawn
questions.explanation: If corresponding angles are congruent, then the lines are parallel. Since both angles measure $65^\circ$ and they occupy corresponding positions, the lines must be parallel.

questions.question: Two lines are cut by a transversal. $m\angle 3 = 110^\circ$ and $m\angle 5 = 70^\circ$. $\angle 3$ and $\angle 5$ are consecutive interior angles. What conclusion follows?
options: The lines are parallel because the angles sum to $180^\circ$
The lines are not parallel because the angles are not congruent
The lines are perpendicular to the transversal
No conclusion can be drawn from consecutive interior angles
questions.explanation: Consecutive interior angles must be supplementary for the lines to be parallel. Since $110^\circ + 70^\circ = 180^\circ$, the condition is satisfied, and the lines are parallel.

questions.question: Two lines are cut by a transversal. $m\angle 4 = 95^\circ$ and $m\angle 8 = 95^\circ$. $\angle 4$ and $\angle 8$ are alternate exterior angles. What conclusion follows?
options: The lines are perpendicular to each other
The lines are parallel
The angles form a linear pair
The transversal is perpendicular to one of the lines
questions.explanation: If alternate exterior angles are congruent, then the lines are parallel by the converse of the Alternate Exterior Angles Theorem. Equal measures of $95^\circ$ satisfy the condition.

questions.question: Two lines are cut by a transversal. $m\angle 1 = 75^\circ$ and $m\angle 7 = 105^\circ$. $\angle 1$ and $\angle 7$ are alternate interior angles. Are the lines parallel?
options: Yes, because the two geometric angles sum to exactly $180^\circ$ across the plane
No, because alternate interior angles must be congruent for the lines to be parallel
Yes, because any two angles cut by a transversal can prove parallelism in this case
No, because the two architectural angles are not both acute or both obtuse on a line
questions.explanation: For lines to be parallel by the converse of the Alternate Interior Angles Theorem, alternate interior angles must be congruent. Here, $75^\circ \neq 105^\circ$, so the condition is not met and parallelism cannot be established from this information alone.

questions.question: Two lines are cut by a transversal. Corresponding angles are found to be congruent. Which additional angle pair must also be congruent as a consequence, without assuming the lines are parallel?
options: Consecutive interior angles
Vertical angles
Angles on opposite sides of the transversal
No other pair must be congruent
questions.explanation: Vertical angles are always congruent regardless of whether the lines are parallel. However, if corresponding angles are congruent and the lines are parallel by the converse, then alternate interior and alternate exterior angles also become congruent as a result.

questions.question: In a proof, a student shows that $m\angle 2 + m\angle 6 = 180^\circ$ and identifies $\angle 2$ and $\angle 6$ as consecutive interior angles. Which reason completes the proof that the lines are parallel?
options: Definition of complementary angles inside a standard intersection model
Converse of the Alternate Interior Angles Theorem within a dynamic plane
Converse of the Consecutive Interior Angles Theorem within a dynamic plane
Corresponding Angles Postulate inside a standard intersection model
questions.explanation: The converse of the Consecutive Interior Angles Theorem states that if consecutive interior angles are supplementary, then the lines are parallel. The sum of $180^\circ$ satisfies this condition.

questions.question: Two lines are cut by a transversal. $\angle A$ and $\angle B$ are alternate interior angles, and $m\angle A = (3x + 10)^\circ$, $m\angle B = (5x - 20)^\circ$. What must $x$ equal for the lines to be parallel?
questions.explanation: For the lines to be parallel, alternate interior angles must be congruent: $3x + 10 = 5x - 20$. Solving: $3x - 5x = -20 - 10$, so $-2x = -30$, giving $x = 15$. Verify: $m\angle A = 3(15) + 10 = 55^\circ$, $m\angle B = 5(15) - 20 = 55^\circ$. ✓

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT a valid method to prove two lines are parallel?
options: Showing that corresponding angles are congruent
Showing that alternate interior angles are congruent
Showing that vertical angles are congruent
Showing that consecutive interior angles are supplementary
questions.explanation: Vertical angles are always congruent for any two intersecting lines, regardless of whether other lines are parallel. This congruence provides no information about parallelism. The other three conditions are all valid converses of parallel line theorems.

scoreMessages.90: Excellent! You can confidently prove lines are parallel using angle relationships and their converses.
scoreMessages.70: Good work! You understand the main methods for proving parallelism but review why vertical angles cannot be used to establish parallel lines.
scoreMessages.50: A fair start. Focus on: three ways to prove lines parallel — congruent corresponding angles, congruent alternate interior angles, congruent alternate exterior angles, or supplementary consecutive interior angles.
scoreMessages.0: Keep practicing! To prove lines are parallel, use converses of the parallel line theorems. Vertical angles and linear pairs are always true regardless of parallelism and cannot be used alone to prove lines are parallel.
